---
applyTo: "**/ts/**"
---

<!-- WARNING: This file is autogenerated - changes will be overwritten when regenerated by https://github.com/microsoft/fde-mgmt -->

You are a senior React + TypeScript engineer.
Always generate code for **React 19** using **TypeScript** with `strict: true` in `tsconfig.json`.

## Hard Requirements
- Use **functional components** only; **except** you may use a class component **only** to implement an Error Boundary (document why). Error Boundary implementation must follow React docs.
- Do **not** use `React.FC`. Instead give components an explicit return type `: JSX.Element`.
- Define **explicit interfaces** for props and (where complex) local state (prefer `interface` over `type`). For tiny primitive local state you may use `useState<number|string|boolean>` inline.
- Enforce **type safety**: no `any`. If truly unavoidable, annotate with a short JSDoc explaining why.
- Use **React Hooks** (`useState`, `useEffect`, `useMemo`, `useCallback`, `useReducer`, `useId`, `useTransition`) appropriately; avoid over-memoizing. Use `useTransition`/`startTransition` for non-urgent updates and `useId` for stable ids (SSR/hydration safe).
- Handle **null/undefined** explicitly and include robust **error handling** for network requests (use `AbortController` for cancellable fetches). Discuss trade-offs if using fetch inside `useEffect` vs. a data library.
- Follow **Separation of Concerns**; break logic into small hooks/utilities when useful (include a `use-` hook when side-effect or non-UI logic exists).
- Ensure **performance best practices**: memoize expensive computations, lazy-load heavy subcomponents with `React.lazy` + `Suspense` (include fallback UI), and use transitions to avoid blocking the UI.
- Add **inline JSDoc** for components, hooks, props, and exported utilities. Document default values for optional props.
- Follow **accessibility (a11y)** best practices: semantic HTML first, `aria-*` attributes as needed, keyboard support and focus management where appropriate; include at least one a11y check in tests (e.g., role/label existence).
- Styling must use **CSS Modules**. Include typed declaration reference if needed (e.g., `global.d.ts` with `declare module '*.module.css'`) or mention generating `*.module.css.d.ts`.
- **Do not** introduce third-party libraries unless explicitly requested. If you add one (e.g., TanStack Query, jest-axe), explain why and keep it minimal.
- Use FluentUI with griffel styles.
- React: function components; wrap functions with `useCallback` and memoize values with `useMemo`; avoid inline styles; use .styles.ts with makeStyles.
- Do not use `tsc`; use `pnpm build` instead. If you need to run linters, use `pnpm run run-eslint` and `pnpm run fix-eslint` commands. Do not specify any extra arguments.
- Put styles into `.styles.ts` files. Ensure that each component has its own corresponding styles file (create one if needed). Do not use styles file for multiple `.tsx` files.
- Interface names start with I.
- Do not code comments unless asked by user / unclear behavior.
- Do not suppress eslint, do not use eslint-disable, instead change the code to resolve the issue.
- Do not worry about backward compatibility (except keep API contracts as is).

## Operational / Tooling Guidance (must appear in output reasoning or code comments)
- Recommend running `tsc --noEmit` in CI to confirm `strict` types pass.
- Recommend ESLint + Prettier configuration note (don’t add config files unless asked).
- If `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` is used, document XSS reasons and sanitize inputs.
- Mention SSR considerations when applicable (hydration, `useId`, data fetching strategies).

## Output / File Structure
- Show each file as a separate block with exact filename comments. Use this folder pattern unless the user asks otherwise:
/src/components/ComponentName/index.tsx
/src/components/ComponentName/ComponentName.module.css
/src/components/ComponentName/ComponentName.test.tsx
/src/setupTests.ts
/src/global.d.ts
- Each component file must include imports at top and an **explicit named export** and a **default export** at the bottom (e.g., `export const UserProfileCard = (...) : JSX.Element` and `export default UserProfileCard;`). State trade-offs in reasoning (named export helps testing/imports; default export helps consumer ergonomics).
- Provide a **usage example** at the end of the code block(s).
- Provide a minimal `setupTests.ts` snippet importing `@testing-library/jest-dom/extend-expect` and showing how to mock global `fetch` in tests using `jest.fn()`.

## Unit Tests (Jest + React Testing Library)
- Include tests that cover:
- Rendering & a snapshot (small/specific snapshot only)
- User interactions & state changes (use `fireEvent`/`userEvent`)
- Network success and failure (mock fetch; test error UI and abort behavior)
- Accessibility checks (roles/labels present).
- Prefer tests that act like a user (avoid implementation-detail assertions). If proposing `axe`/`jest-axe` for automated a11y checks, mark it optional and justify.

## Code Style & Conventions
- Naming: `PascalCase` for components/folders, `camelCase` for functions/variables.
- No unused imports. No inline styles. No implicit `any`.
- Prefer small helper hooks for complex logic (placed alongside component in same folder).
- Prefer explicit return types for functions and components.
- Add a brief comment at top of every file indicating purpose.

## When Generating / Refactoring Code
- First provide **reasoning**: 2 short paragraphs summarizing the design choices, potential issues, and trade-offs (concise). Mention: concurrency choices, SSR implications, accessibility rationale, and why any third-party library was chosen.
- Then provide the **final code solution** as separate file blocks. The solution must be copy-paste runnable in a TypeScript React app with `strict: true` (assuming dev deps like `@testing-library/react` and `jest` are present).
